title: Fizzing bath bombs
subtitle: Citric acid and bicarbonate spheres with essential oil
excerpt: Fizzing bath bombs made from a bicarbonate of soda and citric acid base, pressed into sphere moulds. The classic 2:1 bicarbonate-to-citric ratio gives a strong, sustained fizz. Six bath bombs per batch; each one dissolves in a full bath.
sourceNotes: Bramble Berry bath bomb guide (brambleberry.com, May 2026), citric acid to bicarbonate ratios, binding oil recommendations, humidity management. Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014), lavender bath-use concentrations.

content.text: A fizzing bath bomb. The reaction is simple chemistry: bicarbonate of soda (base) and citric acid (acid) react vigorously in water to produce carbon dioxide gas (the fizz) and a salt solution (sodium citrate). The 2:1 bicarbonate-to-citric ratio is the household standard; it gives a strong sustained fizz without too sharp an acidic feel in the bath water.

Humidity is the enemy of bath bomb making. The dry ingredients begin to react as soon as they absorb moisture from the air; in a humid kitchen (above 50% relative humidity) the mixture will start to fizz in the bowl before you reach the mould. Make bath bombs on a dry day, work quickly, and keep the dry mix in a sealed bowl between press-ins.

items.name: Bicarbonate of soda
items.prepNote: fine bicarbonate of soda (baking soda). 67% of the dry base. Sift before using to remove lumps.

items.name: Citric acid
items.prepNote: fine citric acid powder (food-grade or cosmetic-grade). 33% of the dry base. Sift separately.

items.name: Sweet almond oil
items.prepNote: sweet almond oil. 4% of the batch. The binding oil; adds a light skin-softening quality to the bath water. Carrier oil rather than a heavier butter, so it disperses in the bath rather than sitting on the surface.

items.name: Lavender essential oil
items.prepNote: Lavandula angustifolia lavender essential oil. 2% of the batch. Mixed into the sweet almond oil before drizzling into the dry base.

content.text: Sift the bicarbonate of soda into a large, dry bowl. Sift the citric acid into a separate bowl, then add it to the bicarbonate bowl and stir to combine. Do not use a metal whisk; the friction can trigger a localised reaction. Use a silicone spatula or a wooden spoon.
Mix the wet phase and add to the dry base

Combine the sweet almond oil and the lavender essential oil in a small cup and stir. Drizzle the wet phase into the dry base in a thin stream, a few drops at a time, stirring continuously. The drizzle must be slow; adding the oil too quickly in one spot causes localised fizzing where the citric acid reacts with any trace moisture in the oil. Stir constantly as you drizzle until all the wet phase is incorporated. The dry base will look like damp sand and hold together when pressed between the fingers.

Work quickly from this point. Overfill each half of a sphere mould with the mixture, press together firmly with both palms, then twist slightly as you release so the two halves don't pull apart when you remove the mould. Leave the mould on overnight without touching; attempting to unmould too early will break the bomb in half. After 24 hours the bombs are firm enough to handle. Unmould carefully by flexing the silicone mould or twisting the two halves apart.

Drop one bomb into a full, warm bath. The bomb will fizz actively for 30-60 seconds before dissolving fully. The sweet almond oil and lavender will disperse through the bath water. Soak for 15-20 minutes; rinse afterwards if preferred.

6 months in an airtight container. Wrap each bomb individually in cling film immediately after unmoulding; unwrapped bombs in a humid bathroom will lose their fizzing power within weeks as the citric acid absorbs atmospheric moisture and neutralises the bicarbonate. Discard any bomb that fizzes weakly in the bath.

attrs.intro: The two ways a bath bomb batch commonly fails.

items.fix: Work on a dryer day (below 50% humidity). Add the wet phase more slowly next time. A silica gel packet in the mixing bowl helps in humid conditions.
items.cause: Humidity in the kitchen, or the wet phase was added too quickly in one spot rather than drizzled slowly.
items.symptom: The mixture starts fizzing in the bowl before reaching the mould

items.fix: Leave a further 12 hours before unmoulding. Next batch, add slightly more binding oil (2-3 extra grams) to improve adhesion.
items.cause: Unmoulded too soon (before 24 hours), or the mixture was too dry when pressed.
items.symptom: Bombs crack in half when unmoulding

attrs.heading: If the bath bombs went sideways
